title: Making compost tea to feed plants and improve soil life
subtitle: Aerated and non-aerated methods for turning finished compost into liquid feed
excerpt: Compost tea is finished compost steeped in water to extract soluble nutrients and microbial life. A non-aerated brew (cold water, 24-48 hours) gives a basic liquid feed. An aerated brew (aquarium pump, 24 hours) multiplies beneficial bacteria and produces a more biologically active product.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on liquid feeds and soil improvement. Charles Dowding no-dig gardening method notes on compost applications. Rodale Institute guidance on aerated compost tea (ACT).

glossaryTerms.definition: Water in which finished compost has been steeped to extract soluble nutrients and microbial populations. Applied as a soil drench or foliar spray to feed plants and introduce beneficial microorganisms.

Compost tea brewed with continuous aeration from an aquarium pump. The oxygen supply allows aerobic bacteria to multiply rapidly during the 24-hour brew, producing a more biologically active product than non-aerated steeping.

content.text: is a bucket of water with a handful of finished compost steeped for 24-48 hours. Stir occasionally, strain through cloth or an old pillowcase, and use the liquid as a soil drench around plants. The solids go back onto the compost heap or directly onto soil. This non-aerated method extracts soluble nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium in modest amounts) without significantly multiplying the microbial content.

uses a small aquarium pump to bubble air through the brew continuously. The oxygen allows aerobic bacteria to multiply roughly 1,000-fold in 24 hours, producing a tea that is richer in microbial life. Fill a 10-litre bucket with unchlorinated water (let tap water stand for an hour, or use rainwater). Add 1-2 handfuls of finished compost in a mesh bag or old stocking. Run the pump for 24 hours at room temperature. Strain and apply immediately: the bacteria die rapidly once aeration stops.

Chlorine in mains tap water suppresses the bacterial populations in compost tea. For non-aerated tea this matters less (the brew is not trying to multiply bacteria). For aerated tea, use rainwater if available, or leave tap water in an open bucket for 30-60 minutes before adding compost so the chlorine off-gasses.

Apply compost tea as a soil drench around the root zone of plants: 500 ml-1 litre per established plant, watered in. It can also be used as a dilute foliar spray (dilute the strained tea 1:5 with water) to introduce beneficial microorganisms to leaf surfaces. Apply in the morning so the leaves dry before evening. Do not use as a foliar spray on food crops within one week of harvest.

The tea is only as good as the compost it is made from. Unfinished compost (stringy, undecomposed material, ammonia smell) produces a low-quality tea. Use finished compost that smells earthy, is dark brown, and has no recognisable food or garden waste remaining. A hot compost heap that has completed its active phase and been left for 4-6 weeks to mature produces the best starting material.
